problem_addressed: Fully supervised medical image segmentation requires extensive pixel-level annotations that are costly and time-consuming, making large-scale annotation impractical for clinical workflows.
core_claim: A weakly supervised segmentation framework using only four extreme points as annotation can achieve performance comparable to or surpassing fully supervised methods while significantly reducing annotation cost.
key_contributions: A weakly supervised segmentation framework leveraging only four extreme points as annotation
An enhanced Feature-Guided Extreme Point Masking (FGEPM) algorithm incorporating Monte Carlo dropout-based uncertainty estimation
A dual-branch Uncertainty-aware Scale Consistency (USC) loss and box alignment loss for spatial consistency
novelty: Integration of Monte Carlo dropout-based uncertainty estimation into FGEPM to construct a unified gradient-uncertainty cost map, and the dual-branch USC loss that selectively applies stronger consistency supervision to confident regions while down-weighting ambiguous areas.
method_summary: The framework uses SAM2 with bounding box prompts derived from four extreme points to generate initial pseudo labels, which are progressively refined using an enhanced FGEPM algorithm with Monte Carlo dropout-based uncertainty estimation, while enforcing spatial consistency with USC loss and boundary alignment with box alignment loss.
method_components: Initial pseudo-label generation using SAM2 with bounding box prompts
key_innovation: The construction of a unified gradient-uncertainty cost map using Monte Carlo dropout-based uncertainty estimation within the FGEPM algorithm, which allows contours to be traced more precisely from extreme points and ensures pseudo labels become progressively more accurate during training.
assumptions: Four extreme points (topmost, bottommost, leftmost, rightmost) can be reliably identified as the only annotation
SAM2 can effectively segment lesions when provided with bounding box prompts derived from extreme points
Monte Carlo dropout provides meaningful uncertainty estimates for pixel-wise refinement
theoretical_grounding: The method is grounded in uncertainty-aware consistency designs that enforce agreement on reliable regions while down-weighting high-entropy/variance pixels, reducing the risk of amplifying label noise, though no explicit proofs or bounds are provided.
datasets_used: UNS (Ultrasound Nerve Segmentation) dataset with 2,323 image-mask pairs
hyperparameters: K epochs for pseudo label updates (value not specified), λ and μ for loss balancing (values not specified)
headline_result: Our method achieved 67.70% IoU / 76.89% Dice on BUSI and 67.39% IoU / 79.12% Dice on UNS, surpassing fully supervised performance on BUSI
ablations: SAM2 prompts: +7.43 pp IoU on BUSI, +2.74 pp IoU on UNS
USC Loss & Box Alignment Loss: +9.06 pp IoU on BUSI, +5.24 pp IoU on UNS
UA-FGEPM & Iteration: +9.97 pp IoU on BUSI, +5.26 pp IoU on UNS
improvement_over_baseline: +10.71 pp IoU and +7.27 pp Dice on BUSI, +10.36 pp IoU and +5.19 pp Dice on UNS
stated_limitations: Limited to ultrasound images, may not generalize to other imaging modalities
Requires accurate identification of four extreme points for each structure
Uncertainty estimation relies on Monte Carlo dropout which may have limitations in capturing all sources of uncertainty
unstated_weaknesses: No explicit handling of label noise in the pseudo-label generation process
The method may struggle with complex anatomical structures where four extreme points are insufficient
Potential bias introduced by SAM2's initial pseudo-label generation
Limited evaluation on diverse datasets and imaging conditions
threats_to_validity: Results may not generalize to other medical imaging modalities
Performance may degrade with inaccurate extreme point annotations
Uncertainty estimates might not reflect true model uncertainty
Limited assessment of robustness to varying levels of annotation noise
reproducibility: medium (code availability not specified, but methodology appears implementable with available tools)
open_questions: How does the method perform with fewer or more extreme points?
What is the impact of extreme point localization errors on final segmentation quality?
How well does the uncertainty estimation correlate with actual segmentation errors?
Can the approach be effectively adapted for classification tasks?
future_work: Extending to other imaging modalities beyond ultrasound
Incorporating active learning to improve extreme point selection
Developing more sophisticated uncertainty estimation techniques
Addressing label noise explicitly in the pseudo-label refinement process
gaps_this_enables: Framework for uncertainty-aware pseudo-label generation from sparse annotations
Methodology for progressive refinement of pseudo-labels using uncertainty maps
Potential for adapting extreme point-based annotation to other medical imaging tasks
relevance_to_topic: This paper provides a relevant foundation for uncertainty-aware pseudo-label generation, though it focuses on segmentation rather than classification and doesn't explicitly address label noise.
best_result: Our method achieved 67.70% IoU / 76.89% Dice on BUSI and 67.39% IoU / 79.12% Dice on UNS, surpassing fully supervised performance on BUSI
limitations_stated: Limited to ultrasound images, may not generalize to other imaging modalities
Requires accurate identification of four extreme points for each structure
Uncertainty estimation relies on Monte Carlo dropout which may have limitations in capturing all sources of uncertainty
title: Uncertainty-Aware Extreme Point Tracing for Weakly Supervised Ultrasound Image Segmentation
